candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): guard u32/u16 conversion panics with better error messages
candidates.body: Same concern here: this conversion still panics via `expect`, so out-of-range flags will crash the process. If #4782 is meant to be resolved/closed, this likely needs to become a non-panicking conversion with error propagation (e.g., return `Result<u16, ...>` and surface a parser error) rather than a message-only change.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): guard u32/u16 conversion panics with better error messages
candidates.body: The updated panic message is a bit imprecise: `u32::MAX` is a value, not a type/range. Consider wording like “parser offsets must be <= u32::MAX” (optionally including the actual `value`) so the overflow condition is unambiguous.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): recover invalid arrow conditional tails
candidates.body: The new conditional-tail recovery tests don’t cover the case where the skipped conditional branch contains nested semicolons (e.g. `? (() => { foo(); }) : bar;`). Given the recovery logic scans token-by-token, adding a regression test for a block-bodied arrow/function inside the stray conditional tail would help ensure the scan doesn’t stop early at an inner `;` and still anchors diagnostics at the outer `:`.

candidates.pr_title: perf(docs): T0.4 attribution run + Tier 0 exit decision record
candidates.body: This claim's Approach section says the run will include a representative `large-ts-repo` slice, but this PR’s decision record explicitly defers `large-ts-repo` due to OOM/stack overflow. Please update the claim to match what was actually measured in this PR so the claim file remains an accurate coordination record.

candidates.pr_title: perf(docs): T0.4 attribution run + Tier 0 exit decision record
candidates.body: All `fixture.*` provenance fields are `null` in this checked-in run. Since this data is used as a baseline/decision record, it would be more useful to populate at least `fixture.name` (and ideally repo/ref/path) via the documented `TSZ_BENCH_FIXTURE_*` env vars so the raw JSON remains attributable and comparable later.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): align reachability recovery diagnostics
candidates.body: `pending_const_binding_name_colon` is set when encountering `const`, but it’s only cleared when the *next* token is an identifier/in/out. If `const` is followed by a binding pattern (e.g. `const { a } = …` / `const [a] = …`) or anything else, the flag will remain true and later identifiers can incorrectly trigger a "':' expected." diagnostic far away from the `const`. Consider either (a) lookahead before setting the flag, or (b) clearing it when the next token isn’t a simple binding name, so the state doesn’t leak across unrelated tokens.

candidates.pr_title: fix(solver): preserve literal constraint display candidates
candidates.body: Storing a display alias for `constraint_ty` here can overwrite an existing `display_alias` for the same evaluated constraint type (e.g. the normal alias produced when evaluating `Comparable<number>`). Because `display_alias` is a global interner mapping, this can leak the literal-union provenance into unrelated later diagnostics that happen to format the same `constraint_ty`. Consider avoiding mutation of the global alias map for shared evaluated types (e.g. by attaching the display application to a call-scoped wrapper/diagnostic value, or only storing when you can guarantee the evaluated type was produced specifically for this call site).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve object literal normalization diagnostics
candidates.body: `declared_type_of_identifier_argument` only looks up declaration nodes via `self.ctx.arena.get(decl_idx)`. If the identifier resolves to a symbol declared in a different arena/file (e.g. imported binding, lib declaration, or multi-file binder), these NodeIndex lookups can fail and the function will incorrectly return `None`, skipping the sibling-annotation check. Consider iterating `symbol.stable_declarations`/`stable_value_declaration` and using `ctx.node_at_stable_location(..)` (as done in `call_arg_source_is_typed_identifier`) so typed identifiers are detected across arenas.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): emit TS2315 for explicit `type X = any` aliases
candidates.body: `decl_node` may come from `ctx.arena`, `symbol_arenas`, or `declaration_arenas`, but the subsequent `get_type_alias(decl_node)` calls probe multiple arenas (starting with `ctx.arena`) using a `Node` value whose `data_index` is arena-relative. Because `NodeIndex`/`data_index` values can overlap across arenas, this can accidentally resolve the wrong alias (or the wrong body node) and incorrectly classify unrelated symbols as an explicit-`any` alias. Prefer selecting the correct arena once per `decl_idx` (e.g. via `binder.arena_for_declaration_or(sym_id, decl_idx, self.ctx.arena)`) and then performing *all* lookups (`get`, `get_type_alias`, `get(alias.type_node)`) against that arena.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): emit TS2315 for explicit `type X = any` aliases
candidates.body: The PR description claims conformance has “new failures: 0” and “changed failures: 0”, but the checked-in conformance baseline now includes new failing tests (e.g. `.../excessPropertyCheckIntersectionWithRecursiveType.ts`, `.../narrowingMutualSubtypes.ts`, `.../contextualTypeWithTuple.ts`) and also shows previously failing tests now passing (e.g. `.../maxConstraints.ts`). Please reconcile this with the PR description (update the description or regenerate snapshots from the intended base).

candidates.pr_title: test(checker): cover mapped key TS2322 anchors
candidates.body: `diagnostic_anchor_text` slices `source` using unchecked byte indices derived from `diagnostic.start`/`length`. If a diagnostic ever points into a different file (e.g. lib) or reports a span outside `source`, this will panic with an unhelpful “byte index out of bounds”/“not a char boundary” message. Consider using `source.get(start..end).expect(...)` (and optionally asserting `diagnostic.file == "test.ts"`) so failures produce a clear message and avoid panics on invalid spans.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): harden object normalization review paths
candidates.body: `decl_idx` is obtained from `node_at_stable_location` (potentially from a different arena/file), but `type_of_value_declaration_for_symbol(sym_id, decl_idx)` re-enters the arena-selection logic and can take the wrong fast path if the current arena happens to have a node at the same `NodeIndex` mapped to `sym_id` (cross-arena collision). Since this loop already knows the declaration’s stable location, prefer the explicit cross-file delegation path when `stable_location.file_idx` is stamped / differs from the current file (e.g. `type_of_value_declaration_for_cross_file_symbol`), or otherwise ensure the resolution uses the same arena returned by `node_at_stable_location`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): harden explicit-any alias detection
candidates.body: The new parenthesized-alias test only asserts that TS2315 appears, but it doesn’t assert that cascading TS2344 is suppressed (unlike the canonical explicit-any test above). Adding a `!codes.contains(&2344)` assertion here would prevent regressions where the parenthesized path reintroduces the TS2344 cascade.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): harden explicit-any alias detection
candidates.body: This file’s recorded failing set changes (adds/removes failures) even though the PR’s verification doesn’t mention re-running conformance. If the intent is only checker hardening + unit tests, consider reverting this regenerated detail; otherwise, please note the conformance snapshot command used so reviewers can trust the updated data.

candidates.pr_title: docs(solver): clarify interner perf-counter cost summary
candidates.body: The wording "the optimizer folds into the no-op path" overstates what the compiler can guarantee here. Since `pc` depends on a runtime `enabled_fast()` result, the `if let Some(c) = pc` checks still compile to an Option discriminant test + branch; they’re just predictable/cheap when `pc` is `None`. Consider rephrasing to describe the actual runtime shape (predictable branch, body skipped), without implying it is optimized away.

candidates.pr_title: test(checker): cover max constraints diagnostic display
candidates.body: This test picks the first diagnostic with code 2345 via `find`, which doesn’t guarantee it’s the diagnostic for `max2(1, 2)` if additional TS2345s ever appear (ordering can change as the checker evolves). To make the test unambiguous, filter TS2345 diagnostics and select the one whose `start` matches `first_arg_start` (or assert there’s exactly one TS2345 before using it).

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): recover incomplete constructor annotations
candidates.body: `recover_orphan_case_assignment_before_if` currently advances tokens until `;` or EOF only. If the orphan `case = ...` statement is terminated by ASI (line break) or the end of a block (`}`) without an explicit semicolon, this loop can consume the following statement/block close and prevent correct recovery. Consider also stopping on `CloseBraceToken` and/or a preceding line break boundary while skipping the orphan assignment tail.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve conditional keyof variance
candidates.body: The test only asserts that there are 8 TS2322 diagnostics, but it would still pass if additional non-TS2322 diagnostics are emitted (which would indicate a regression unrelated to variance). To ensure this remains a tight TSC-conformance check, also assert that the total diagnostics count is 8 (or that all diagnostics are TS2322) so unexpected errors can’t slip through.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve higher-order generic call inference
candidates.body: `collect_all_types` returns an `FxHashSet`, so iterating it here makes the order of `hoisted` (and therefore the synthesized function `type_params`) depend on hash iteration order. Since `type_params` ordering is part of the `FunctionShape` identity and affects diagnostics, this can introduce non-deterministic behavior/flaky snapshots. Consider collecting the referenced `__infer_src_*` params into a Vec and sorting deterministically (e.g., by `Atom`/name) before assigning to `shape.type_params`.

candidates.pr_title: docs(checker): clarify cross-file-query helper module docstring
candidates.body: The module-level docstring implies that the helpers in this module collapse `TypeId::UNKNOWN` to `None`, but that isn’t true for all helpers here (e.g. `cached_cross_file_class_instance_type` explicitly does *not* filter `ERROR`/`UNKNOWN`/`ANY`; see the note on that function). Consider tightening this wording to refer specifically to the `cached_cross_file_symbol_type`/`SymbolType` path (or say “some/most helpers”) so the module docs don’t conflict with the behavior documented below.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): widen overflow tuple boolean display
candidates.body:
`target_element` is looked up only via `elements.get(element_position)`, so for tuple targets with a rest element (variadic tuples) any element past `elements.len()` is treated as “overflow” with no contextual slot. That loses the fact those positions are still contextually typed by the tuple’s rest element, which can change the intended literal-preservation / display behavior.

Consider resolving `target_element` for out-of-range positions via the tuple’s rest element (e.g. `elements.iter().rev().find(|e| e.rest)`) so rest-backed positions are treated as target-backed too.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): contextually type empty arrays in logical OR
candidates.body: This new regression test relies on standard-library utility types (`Omit`, `Partial`), but `check_source_diagnostics` runs with lib definitions disabled (see `test_utils.rs`), so the snippet will produce TS2304/TS2552-style “cannot find name” errors and the test can still pass because it only asserts on TS2345. Define minimal `Partial`/`Omit` (and any dependencies like `Pick`/`Exclude`) in the snippet, or broaden the assertion to fail on unexpected diagnostics so the test actually validates the intended behavior.

candidates.pr_title: fix(binder): guard persistent scope count overflow with better error message
candidates.body: This still panics on overflow via `expect(...)` (only the message changed). Since the PR description/linked issue describes the crash as the bug to fix (and suggests returning/propagating an internal error or hard-capping), consider making this path non-panicking (e.g., change this API to return `Result` and bubble an internal diagnostic) or update the PR/issue linkage to reflect that this is only an improved panic message, not a behavioral fix.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): guard u32/u16 conversion panics with better error messages
candidates.body: These helpers still use `try_from(...).expect(...)`, so inputs above `u32::MAX` will continue to hard-panic (now with a different message). Since this PR claims to close #4782 (panic vs recoverable bounds error), consider changing this helper to return a `Result`/`Option` and converting the overflow into a parser diagnostic (or another non-panicking error path) instead of panicking.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): guard NodeArena::len_u32 overflow with better error message
candidates.body: There are existing tests elsewhere that assert overflow panic messages contain a specific constant (e.g., `SOURCE_FILE_LEN_OVERFLOW_MESSAGE` in `crates/tsz-core/src/source_file/mod.rs`). Since this PR changes the NodeArena overflow message, it would be good to add a similar regression test in this file’s `#[cfg(test)]` module to lock in the new wording and avoid accidental message regressions.

candidates.pr_title: Fix generic union in-operand diagnostics
candidates.body: The TS2322 assertion is order-dependent on the printed union type (`"T | { a: string; }"`). Union member ordering appears to be non-deterministic elsewhere in the test suite (some tests accept either `A | B` or `B | A`), so this can make the test flaky. Consider matching both orderings or asserting on smaller substrings (e.g., that the message contains both `"T"` and `"{ a: string; }"`) instead of the full union rendering.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): widen overflow tuple boolean display
candidates.body: `target_element` is computed via `elements.get(element_position)`, which only works for fixed tuple slots. For target tuples with a `...rest` element, positions beyond `elements.len()` are still contextually typed by the rest element, but this code will treat them as overflow and force the widened fallback (dropping `true`/`false` preservation and skipping `literal_expression_display`). Consider resolving the contextual element with a rest fallback (e.g., when `element_position >= len`, use the last `rest` element if present) so rest-typed positions behave like other contextually typed tuple slots, while fixed-length overflow still widens.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): cover intersection index signature display
candidates.body: The TS2322 assertions here use full string equality for the entire diagnostic message. In this repo, TS2322 tests typically assert on key substrings (e.g., `contains(...)`) to avoid brittleness from incidental formatting changes (property ordering, spacing/semicolon tweaks, or additional details). Consider loosening this to substring checks that still guarantee the intersection + alias surfaces you care about.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve enum member union diagnostics
candidates.body: `format_union_with_collapsed_enum_display` now pushes formatted enum member names into `rendered` but still returns `None` unless `collapsed_enum` is set. For unions composed entirely of enum members (the case this change targets), this causes the code to fall back to the diagnostic `TypeFormatter`, which (per `tsz-solver/src/diagnostics/format/compound.rs:909+`) collapses same-enum member unions back to the parent enum name, so the member names won’t actually be preserved. Consider returning `Some(rendered.join(" | "))` when you’ve rendered any enum member names (or otherwise short-circuiting before the formatter runs) so the preservation behavior takes effect.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align nested async class expression emit
candidates.body: `NON_NULL_EXPRESSION` nodes are not type assertions, so `self.arena.get_type_assertion(node)` will always return `None` here. If you want to unwrap `expr!`, use the unary accessor (e.g. `get_unary_expr_ex`) to reach the inner `expression` instead of `get_type_assertion`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align jsxComponentTypeErrors TS2786 fingerprint
candidates.body: The test name says TS2786 should be emitted **at** `MixedComponent`, but the assertion only checks the message text. To catch the fingerprint/anchor regression this PR is about, consider also asserting `diag.start` (and/or `length`) matches the `MixedComponent` tag name location (similar to other anchor-focused tests in this file).

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): wrap automatic JSX runtime in system modules
candidates.body: In System `react-jsxdev` emit, `jsx_dev_file_name` is only set when it is currently `None`. If the same `Printer` instance is reused across multiple files, this can reuse a previous file’s `_jsxFileName` value for later emits. Consider setting `jsx_dev_file_name` unconditionally for the current source (and restoring afterward) instead of only when `None`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align recursiveFunctionTypes display fingerprints
candidates.body: `should_elide_recursive_typeof_function_return` currently returns true for any nested Function/Callable return type that contains a `TypeQuery` anywhere in its signature, regardless of whether it’s actually part of a self-referential recursion. That means diagnostic-mode formatting can collapse non-recursive higher-order returns to `...` as well. If the intent is to match tsc’s recursive-cycle elision only, consider tightening the predicate (or adding a regression test proving non-recursive `typeof` nested returns should/shouldn’t elide).

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align decl file comment placement
candidates.body: `dot_pos` is derived via a raw byte search for `'.'` in the source span. This can match a `.` inside a block/line comment between the base expression and the real property-access dot (e.g. `obj/*has . here*/.prop`), which can cause the split comment emission to write parts of the original source (including the real `.`) via `leading_text`, resulting in duplicated dots / incorrect spacing and mapping. Consider locating the dot token while skipping comment ranges (similar to `source_tail_contains_equals_outside_comments`) or otherwise ensuring the found `.` is outside comments/trivia.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align decl file comment placement
candidates.body: The new regression test is named `decl_file_...` but uses `emit_js()` without enabling declaration emit (`set_declaration_emit(true)`). As written, it only asserts JS emit behavior and won’t catch regressions specific to `.d.ts` emission that the PR description focuses on. Either enable declaration emit in the helper/test or rename the test to reflect that it targets JS/type-erasure emit.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): report JS @type subclass assignment mismatch
candidates.body: The raw initializer re-check in the `jsdoc_new_expression_relation` branch clears the initializer cache and then calls `get_type_of_node_with_request(..., TypingRequest::NONE)`, which will repopulate `ctx.node_types` for the initializer with the non-contextual (raw) type. That overwrites the earlier contextually-typed initializer entry you intentionally seeded into `node_types` (to prevent later context-free lookups from widening/losing inference), and can change downstream behavior/fingerprints. Consider preserving/restoring the contextual cached initializer type after the raw check (e.g., save the current cached type and re-insert `init_type`), or compute the raw type under a snapshot/rollback that also restores `node_types`/request caches rather than only diagnostics.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): inline semicolon class typedef tags
candidates.body: This change introduces new behavior for resolving `@type {Name}` on class properties via nearby semicolon-only `@typedef` tags, but there’s no regression test covering the semicolon-class-element scenario. Please add a focused test case (similar to the existing JSDoc/type alias tests) that demonstrates the previous leak and asserts the inlined base type in the emitted `.d.ts`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): emit js function type tag signatures
candidates.body: Parameter splitting in `parse_jsdoc_function_type_signature` uses `params_text.split(',')`, which will split inside nested types that contain commas (tuples, generics, function types, object types, etc.) and can produce incorrect `name/type` pairs that still pass validation, leading to invalid emitted signatures. Consider reusing the existing `split_jsdoc_params` + `parse_function_type_param_decl`/`find_top_level_colon` helpers (already used elsewhere in this file) to split only on top-level commas and locate the top-level colon safely.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): preserve generic jsdoc type tags
candidates.body: `jsdoc_generic_name_like_type_reference` will return true for the legacy JSDoc dot-generic form `Array.<T>` because the base `Array.` passes the current base-name character filter. Since `jsdoc_name_like_type_expr_for_pos` later emits the expression verbatim, this can produce invalid `.d.ts` output (`Array.<string>` is not valid TypeScript; it should be `Array<string>`). Consider rejecting bases that end with `.` (or normalizing by stripping the trailing dot before `<`) and add a regression test for `@type {Array.<string>}`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): decorate system class exports
candidates.body: `emit_system_legacy_class_decorator_export` emits mapped output, then truncates and rewrites it. `SourceWriter::truncate` recomputes line/column but does not roll back any source-map mappings already added, so this can produce incorrect sourcemaps when source map generation is enabled. Prefer emitting into a temporary buffer (and merging mappings with offsets), or avoid speculative emission/truncation for this path when sourcemaps are enabled.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): decorate system class exports
candidates.body: The assertion for the `__decorate` helper only checks that the helper text exists somewhere in the output, but the test message claims it is inlined inside the `System.register` callback. To make this a regression test for placement, consider asserting relative ordering (e.g., helper appears after `System.register(... function (exports_1, context_1) {` and the callback's `"use strict";`).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align indexSignatures1 fingerprints
candidates.body: This rewrite unconditionally appends new `Diagnostic::error` entries without checking whether an equivalent diagnostic (same code/start/length/message) already exists. If the checker later starts emitting any of these diagnostics naturally, this function will introduce duplicates and break conformance again. Consider making the rewrite idempotent by rewriting existing matching diagnostics in-place or by checking for an existing `(start, code)` (and possibly message) before pushing.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align indexSignatures1 fingerprints
candidates.body: The span lookup falls back to `source_text.find(anchor_marker)` if `line_marker` isn't found. That can attach a newly injected diagnostic to the wrong occurrence of the anchor (e.g. a declaration earlier in the file) if the test file changes slightly. It would be safer to skip emitting that diagnostic when the `line_marker` isn't found (or to search relative to a more stable surrounding marker) rather than falling back to a potentially unrelated match.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): defer parameter-dependent recursive alias TS2589
candidates.body: The claim format in docs/plan/claims/README.md specifies Status values like `claim`, `ready`, `shipped`, `abandoned`. Using `in progress` here appears to deviate from that convention and may break any tooling/grep workflows that expect the documented values.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): cover intersection index signature display
candidates.body: Same concern as the previous TS2322 test: asserting full message equality is likely to be brittle for TS2322 diagnostics. Prefer checking specific required substrings (e.g., that the source prints as an intersection and that the target prints as an index signature) while allowing other diagnostic text to evolve.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): decorate system class exports
candidates.body: This inlines only `DECORATE_HELPER`, but legacy decorator emit can also reference `__param` (constructor/method parameter decorators) and `__metadata` (when `emit_decorator_metadata` is enabled). In the no-transform System wrapper path, those helpers won’t be emitted elsewhere, so decorated output can call `__param`/`__metadata` without definitions. Consider emitting the full required legacy-decorator helper set (decorate/param/metadata) based on a scan (or a shared helper-needed computation) rather than only `__decorate`.

candidates.pr_title: chore(emitter,binder-tests): share parse_test_source helper across src-mounted test modules
candidates.body: `parse_test_source` takes `&str` and then immediately allocates via `source.to_string()`. In tests that already build a `String` (e.g. the joined `source` used later in this module), this introduces an extra clone/allocation versus moving the existing `String`. Consider adding a variant that takes `String`, or making the helper generic over `Into<String>` and adjusting call sites that don't need to retain the original string.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): guard u32/u16 conversion panics with better error messages
candidates.body: As with the u32 helper, the phrase “must fit in u16::MAX” is awkward/ambiguous because `u16::MAX` is a single value. Consider “must be <= u16::MAX” and/or include the actual `value` that overflowed for easier debugging.

candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): recover invalid arrow conditional tails
candidates.body: In `skip_invalid_conditional_branch_to_colon`, the loop stops when encountering any `SemicolonToken` (see the `while !matches!(..., EndOfFileToken | SemicolonToken)` condition). This will prematurely terminate scanning if the true-branch contains a nested semicolon (e.g. a block-bodied arrow/function expression `? (() => { foo(); }) : ...`), leaving the parser positioned at an inner `;` and preventing recovery from finding the outer `:`. Consider treating `;` as a terminator only when `paren_depth == 0 && brace_depth == 0 && bracket_depth == 0` (similar to `skip_invalid_conditional_branch_to_statement_end`), and otherwise continuing to scan.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): expand index-signature alias receiver display
candidates.body: `diagnostic_messages` calls `load_default_lib_files()` on every invocation, and this helper is used by multiple tests in this file. Since `load_default_lib_files()` reads and parses lib assets from disk, consider caching the loaded libs (e.g., a `OnceLock<Vec<Arc<LibFile>>>`) so the test suite doesn’t repeatedly reload the same lib bundle.

candidates.pr_title: fix(cli): anchor TS2578 at comment range, not at line start
candidates.body: `find_directive_in_text` still returns a `(DirectiveKind, usize)` but the `usize` (relative offset) is no longer used now that `directive_text_start` was removed. Consider changing the helper to return only `DirectiveKind` (or otherwise using the offset) to avoid carrying an unused value through the API and to make the intent clearer.

candidates.pr_title: fix(cli): anchor TS2578 at comment range, not at line start
candidates.body: The PR description claims “new failures: 0” and a category delta of `false_positive 0`, but the updated conformance snapshot shows `false_positive: 1` with `TS2589` listed under extra/false-positive codes. Please either update the PR description to reflect the new TS2589 false positive (and any related baseline/detail changes) or regenerate the conformance artifacts if this is unintended/stale.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve literal constraint display for generic calls
candidates.body: The candidate deduplication uses `candidates.contains(&candidate)` inside the loop, which makes this O(n^2) in the number of literal candidates. Consider tracking seen candidates with a small `FxHashSet`/`IndexSet` (or similar) while building `candidates` to keep this linear and simpler to reason about as argument counts grow (e.g., large rest-arg calls).

candidates.body: This committed raw JSON includes an absolute user-specific path in `command_line[0]` (`/Users/.../.target/release/tsz`). candidates.pr_title: fix(parser): align reachability recovery diagnostics
candidates.body: Inside the `for await` header recovery, `pending_for_expression_comma` remains true after emitting the first `"," expected."` on an identifier (it’s only cleared on the closing `)` case). This means a complex `of` expression with multiple identifiers (e.g. `foo + bar`) will emit the same diagnostic repeatedly on each identifier token, which can spam diagnostics and shift anchors. Consider tracking whether the expression-start comma has already been reported (or clearing the flag after the first emission) while still keeping the separate close-paren diagnostic if needed.

candidates.pr_title: fix(solver): preserve literal constraint display candidates
candidates.body: `un_widened.clone()` allocates just to build a union while `un_widened` is still needed later in the fallback branch. Since the interner has `union_from_slice`, you can avoid the clone by building the union from `&un_widened` (or otherwise restructure to compute `candidate_type` once).

candidates.pr_title: fix(solver): preserve literal constraint display candidates
candidates.body: `let mut display_subst = final_subst.clone()` clones the entire substitution map in a hot path (constraint validation) just to override a single binding for display-only instantiation. If possible, avoid the full clone by temporarily shadowing `tp.name` (save/restore) or by adding a helper that can instantiate with a single-name override layered on top of an existing substitution.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve object literal normalization diagnostics
candidates.body: `display_has_boolean_member_literal_assignability` uses a raw substring search (": true"/": false"), which can produce false positives when those characters appear inside string literal member types (e.g. `{ x: "foo: true" }`). This can incorrectly trigger the normalized-union display preservation. Consider using the same colon+space token scan as `display_has_member_literals_assignability` (checking the post-": " segment starts with `true`/`false` and is not within quotes), or otherwise parsing in a way that only matches boolean literals as member values.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve object literal normalization diagnostics
candidates.body: Inside the per-property loop, this re-computes `evaluate_type_for_assignability(target)` and re-fetches display properties for every property. Since `target` is constant for the whole call, consider hoisting `let evaluated = self.evaluate_type_for_assignability(target);` and the `get_display_properties(..)` lookup outside the loop to avoid repeated work in this hot diagnostic path.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): defer parameter-dependent recursive alias TS2589
candidates.body: `conditional_body_has_unresolved_computed_recursive_alias_ref(...)` is called unconditionally here, even when `body_is_conditional` is false. Since the TS2589 definition-site check is intended to apply only to conditional aliases, consider gating this computation behind `body_is_conditional` (or otherwise ensuring the unresolved-computed path can’t trigger for non-conditional aliases).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): emit TS2315 for explicit `type X = any` aliases
candidates.body: `arena_ref` selection uses `self.ctx.arena.get(alias.type_node).is_some()` to guess which arena owns `alias.type_node`, but `NodeIndex` values are not globally unique across arenas. This can pick `ctx.arena` even when the alias came from a lib/declaration arena, leading to reading an unrelated node at the same index. Consider carrying the `decl_arena` used to read the alias forward (or using `arena_for_declaration_or`) instead of probing by index presence.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): emit TS2315 for explicit `type X = any` aliases
candidates.body: The explicit-`any` detection doesn't unwrap wrapped type nodes (notably `syntax_kind_ext::PARENTHESIZED_TYPE`). For `type X = (any)` (or nested parentheses), `alias.type_node` will be a wrapped type whose inner is the `TypeReference` to `any`, so this helper will return false and TS2315 will still be suppressed. Consider skipping through `PARENTHESIZED_TYPE` via `get_wrapped_type` (looping as needed) before checking for `TYPE_REFERENCE`/`AnyKeyword`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): defer parameter-dependent recursive alias TS2589
candidates.body: `conditional_body_has_unresolved_computed_recursive_alias_ref` is now evaluated unconditionally for every type alias, even when `has_stable_recursive_ref` is false. This is an extra full AST walk compared to the previous behavior and could become noticeable on large codebases; consider gating it behind a cheap precheck (e.g. only when the body is a conditional type and/or when the body contains any self-reference) so non-recursive aliases don't pay the cost.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): defer parameter-dependent recursive alias TS2589
candidates.body: The new stability check only looks for identifiers in `type_parameter_scope` via `type_arg_nodes_contain_scoped_type_parameter_for_depth_check`, but `type_node_is_deferred_passthrough_for_depth_check` also treats enclosing `infer` bindings as deferred. This means a recursive call like `Foo<Wrap<U>>` (where `U` is from `infer U`) will be treated as a stable recursive reference because `Wrap<U>` isn't a passthrough node and `U` isn't in `type_parameter_scope`, potentially reintroducing definition-site TS2589 false positives for common conditional-type patterns that wrap `infer` results. Consider extending the "contains scoped parameter" check here to also treat `infer`-bound identifiers as dependencies (e.g. a recursive `type_node_contains_deferred_passthrough_identifier_for_depth_check` that recurses and checks both type params and infer bindings).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve unique symbol keys in keyof
candidates.body: `property_name_to_key_type` treats symbol-named properties as string-literal keys unless the name is a synthetic `__unique_<id>` atom. This means well-known computed symbol keys like `[Symbol.iterator]` (which are marked `is_symbol_named`) will currently contribute a *string* literal key to `keyof`, so `Extract<keyof T, string>` can incorrectly keep symbol keys. Consider mapping non-`__unique_` symbol-named properties to a non-string key type (e.g. `symbol`, or a `UniqueSymbol` when possible) rather than `literal_string_atom`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve unique symbol keys in keyof
candidates.body: `precompute_computed_property_names` currently encodes every `unique symbol` computed name as a synthetic `"__unique_<id>"` atom. Elsewhere (e.g. `get_property_name_resolved`) unique symbols that are members of the global `Symbol` object are canonicalized to `[Symbol.<name>]` to keep names stable across different syntactic forms. Without the same canonicalization here, computed names like `[observable]` where `observable: typeof Symbol.iterator` can lower to `__unique_<id>` and fail to match members lowered as `[Symbol.iterator]`. Consider reusing the same `[Symbol.<name>]` canonicalization when the `SymbolRef` resolves to a `Symbol.*` member.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve unique symbol keys in keyof
candidates.body: `precompute_symbol_named_computed_property_names` re-traverses the same declarations as `precompute_computed_property_names` and recomputes `get_type_of_node` for each computed property expression. This duplicates potentially expensive work during interface lowering. Consider folding symbol-named metadata into `precompute_computed_property_names` (e.g. returning both the name map and a symbol-named set/flag) to avoid a second full pass.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): report class-expression field this misses
candidates.body: `get_type_of_node_with_request` already performs class-expression member checking in `dispatch.rs` (and intentionally skips it in re-entrant cases via `class_constructor_resolution_set`). Calling `check_direct_class_expression_initializer` unconditionally here can (a) double-check the same class expression and duplicate diagnostics, and (b) bypass the re-entrancy guard that avoids false TS2339s. Consider guarding this call to only run when the dispatch path would have skipped member checking (re-entrant), or refactor the dispatch/class-expression typing path so the needed "this miss" diagnostics are reported without a second pass.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): report class-expression field this misses
candidates.body: This new cached-instance-`this` guard avoids returning `ANY`/`ERROR` for the general instance-member path, but the earlier explicit `this: CurrentClass` fast path still returns `cached_instance_this_type` without filtering. Since `cached_instance_this_type` can be set to `TypeId::ANY` (e.g., cycle-breaking in `get_class_instance_type`), the explicit-`this` case can still incorrectly return `ANY` and hide downstream diagnostics. Apply the same `ANY`/`ERROR` filtering (or `type_contains_error`) to the explicit-`this` cached return as well.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): cover intersection index signature display
candidates.body: PR description mentions adding test coverage for "max constraints diagnostic display", but this change set (as reflected in this diff) only adds intersection/index-signature display tests. Either add the max-constraints coverage in this PR or update the PR description so it matches the actual changes.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): preserve numeric union aliases in TS2322
candidates.body: `declared_numeric_literal_union_alias_source_display` lookup chain is repeated here (and again later when computing `source_from_annotation`). Consider computing the declared-display once and reusing it to avoid duplicated AST queries/type evaluation during diagnostic rendering and reduce the chance of the two call sites drifting in behavior.

candidates.pr_title: fix(cli): allow emit after removed import flags
candidates.body: `is_removed_option_diagnostic_code` currently returns true for both TS5102 (removed option key) and TS5108 (removed option value like `target=ES3`). The function name suggests only "removed option" keys; consider renaming to reflect that it also covers removed *values* (or split into two helpers) to avoid future misuse.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): recover namespace function arrow bodies
candidates.body: `parse_function_declaration` now stores the recovered `=>` expression in `FunctionData.body` (and marks `equals_greater_than_token`). Downstream emitters still treat function-declaration bodies as blocks (e.g., they write `function f(...) ` and then emit `body`), so a non-block body will lead to invalid JS output outside the namespace-special-cases added in this PR. Consider representing this recovery separately (e.g., a dedicated recovery field/kind), or ensure the general function-declaration emit path detects `=>`-recovery (via `equals_greater_than_token` and/or `body.kind != BLOCK`) and emits the expression as an expression statement (matching tsc) instead of emitting a function declaration.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): recover namespace function arrow bodies
candidates.body: `IRNode::ExpressionStatement` printing currently only parenthesizes `FunctionExpr`, but the recovered arrow body can be any expression (notably an object literal like `{ a: 1 }`). Emitting that as a bare expression statement would produce `{ a: 1 };` (a block with a label) instead of `({ a: 1 });`. Either wrap object-literal/function expressions in parentheses when constructing this IR node, or update the IR printer’s `ExpressionStatement` emission to apply the same disambiguation rules as the AST emitter.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): recover namespace function arrow bodies
candidates.body: `emit_recovered_namespace_function_arrow_body` emits the recovered body via `emit_expression(...)` directly. That bypasses the expression-statement disambiguation logic (parenthesizing leading `{` / `function` / etc.) used by `emit_expression_statement`, so cases like `function f() => { a: 1 }` would likely emit `{ a: 1 };` (parsed as a block) instead of an object-literal expression statement. Consider reusing the same paren/disambiguation rules as `emit_expression_statement` when printing the recovered expression in statement position.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align objectRestNegative rest assignment fingerprint
candidates.body: This replaces the centralized `check_assignable_or_report_at_exact_anchor` pipeline with a manual `is_assignable_to` + one-line TS2322. That bypasses the normal “explain” path and can drop related-information elaboration (e.g. “Types of property 'a' are incompatible.”), as well as other assignability-special cases handled by the reporter (weak-type TS2559 substitution, exactOptionalPropertyTypes variants, etc.). Consider emitting via the standard failure rendering while still forcing the top-level display types to come from the computed `rest_type`/`rest_target_type` (e.g. render the solver failure reason with explicit display types) so we keep both the correct fingerprint and the richer diagnostic details.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align nested async class expression emit
candidates.body: The extra `IRNode::VarDecl` uses `self.generate_temp_name()` but that name is not wired to the temp alias actually used when printing the nested static class expression initializer. If the transformer's temp counter has already advanced (e.g., due to computed property temps or `current_static_class_alias`), this can emit `var _b;` while the nested class-expression emitter still generates `(_a = ...)`, causing an undeclared temp (or an unused var) in the output. Consider deriving the alias name using the same temp-naming scheme as the class-expression emitter and passing it through, or avoid emitting a standalone `var` unless you can guarantee it matches the emitted alias.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align nested async class expression emit
candidates.body: `avoid_generator_state_collision` is currently hard-coded to only rewrite `function (_a)` when `class_temp == "_a"`, and it only replaces `_a.label` / `_a.sent()`. The async lowering can choose other generator-state names (via `IRPrinter::generator_state_name_for_hoisted`), and generator bodies can reference the state object via other properties (e.g. `_a.trys`, `_a.ops`, etc.). This string-based replacement can therefore miss collisions or produce mixed `_a`/`_b` state references. Consider reusing/porting the structured renaming logic from `IRPrinter::rename_colliding_outer_generator_state` (which detects the actual state param name, avoids nested functions, and rewrites all `<state>.` uses), and make it work for any colliding temp name, not just `_a`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): wrap automatic JSX runtime in system modules
candidates.body: System wrapper emission now forces the automatic JSX path to emit `_jsx(...)` / `_jsxDEV(...)` when `original_module_kind` is `System`, but I can't find any code in the System wrapper that declares or initializes `_jsx`/`_jsxDEV`/`_jsxs`/`_Fragment` (there’s no JSX auto-import text emission in `emit_system_execute_body`, and `add_system_jsx_runtime_dependency` only assigns the runtime module temp). This will produce output that can throw `ReferenceError: _jsx is not defined` at runtime. Consider either (a) keeping the member-call form for System (`(0, jsx_runtime_1.jsx)(...)`) or (b) extending the System dependency plan/setter emission to bind the needed helpers (e.g. `_jsx = jsx_runtime_1_1.jsx`, `_Fragment = jsx_runtime_1_1.Fragment`, etc.) and hoist those helper names as vars based on `scan_jsx_usage()`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): wrap automatic JSX runtime in system modules
candidates.body: `emit_system_execute_body` always emits an `_jsxFileName = ...;` assignment in `react-jsxdev` mode once `jsx_dev_file_name` is set, but this isn’t gated on actual JSX runtime usage / presence of a `.../jsx-dev-runtime` dependency. For a System module compiled with `jsx=react-jsxdev` but containing no JSX, this will still emit the assignment while `_jsxFileName` is never hoisted, leading to a runtime `ReferenceError`. Gate this emission on `scan_jsx_usage()` or on whether the System dependency plan includes a `/jsx-dev-runtime` action (similar to the hoist logic in `collect_system_hoisted_names`).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align recursiveFunctionTypes display fingerprints
candidates.body: `direct_param_display` is computed before `overloaded_recursive_typeof_parameter_display`, but in the early-return path it’s unused. Since `format_type_diagnostic` can be relatively expensive, consider moving the `direct_param_display` computation below this new fast-path (or compute it lazily only when needed).

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): align decl file comment placement
candidates.body: This new `skip_comments_in_range(node.pos, open_paren_pos)` will consume *all* comments between the start of the function declaration and the opening `(`, including comments that appear after the function name but before `(` (e.g. `function foo /*c*/(x) {}`). If the intent is only to drop comments in the erased pre-name span, consider narrowing the skipped range (or emitting comments in-place between the name end and `(`) to avoid silently deleting user comments in JS output.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): expand returned rest tuple aliases
candidates.body: Unlabeled tuple elements (e.g. `[string, number]`) are valid TS, but expansion currently requires `name: type` labels via `split_once(':')`, so it will fail and fall back to the unexpanded rest param. Consider synthesizing stable parameter names for unlabeled tuple elements so this expansion works for common tuple forms.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): expand returned rest tuple aliases
candidates.body: `unique_parameter_name` only de-dupes within the expanded tuple elements. If earlier parameters in the same signature already use a tuple-element label (e.g. an existing `x` param and a tuple element also labeled `x`), expansion can emit duplicate parameter names. Seed the `seen` list with previously-emitted parameter names when expanding so names are unique across the whole parameter list.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): accept React component type unions
candidates.body: The union-member early-return isn’t gated by `!is_this_tag`, so `<this />` could be incorrectly treated as valid when its `component_type` is a union whose members allow props extraction. This contradicts the earlier “Keep `this` tags strict” behavior—consider adding the same `!is_this_tag` guard around this union shortcut.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emitter): report symlinked import-ref TS2883
candidates.body: This test only cleans up the temp directory at the very end. If any `expect!`/`assert_eq!` above fails, the temp tree is left behind under the system temp dir. Consider using a guard (e.g., `tempfile::TempDir` or a `Drop` cleanup guard) so cleanup runs even when the test fails/panics.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): emit TS7008 for JS null constructor members
candidates.body: The verification command includes an absolute local path (`/Users/...`) which is machine-specific and embeds a username. Prefer a repo-relative path, a variable (e.g. `$TS_TESTS_DIR`), or a short note describing the invocation without hardcoding a local filesystem path.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): inline semicolon class typedef tags
candidates.body: `local_semicolon_class_member_typedef_type_text` builds a `Vec` of matching comments (`collect::<Vec<_>>()`) just to iterate in reverse. This adds an allocation and walks the full comments list for every `@type` on a property. Consider scanning `self.all_comments.iter().rev()` directly (skipping comments with `end > cursor`) and updating `cursor` inside the loop, which preserves the current semantics without allocating.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): inline semicolon class typedef tags
candidates.body: `is_simple_jsdoc_type_name` duplicates the identifier check logic that already exists as `is_jsdoc_property_identifier_name` later in this module. Consider reusing the existing helper (or extracting a shared one) to avoid two subtly divergent implementations over time.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): emit js function type tag signatures
candidates.body: `parse_jsdoc_function_type_signature` finds the parameter list close-paren via `after_params.find(')')`, which stops at the first `)` and will mis-parse valid function types when a parameter type contains nested parentheses (e.g. callbacks like `(cb: (x: T) => void) => void`). This can lead to emitting an invalid `.d.ts` signature rather than safely falling back. Use the existing `find_matching_paren` (already in this module) or another balanced-paren scan to locate the matching `)` for the top-level parameter list before extracting `params_text`/`return_type`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): preserve generic jsdoc type tags
candidates.body: `jsdoc_generic_name_like_type_reference` allows quotes in generic arguments, but `jsdoc_angle_brackets_are_balanced` counts `<`/`>` even inside quoted string literals. This can incorrectly reject valid types like `Test<"a>b">` (the `>` in the literal will underflow the depth), causing the generic reference to not be preserved. Either remove quote support here or update the balance check to ignore angle brackets inside quoted strings (with escape handling).

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): preserve generic jsdoc type tags
candidates.body: `trim_jsdoc_same_line_following_tags` only looks for the substring `" @"`. If the next tag is separated by a tab (e.g. `@template T\t@typedef ...`) or other whitespace patterns, the `@typedef` portion will not be trimmed and can be mis-parsed as template parameters. Consider scanning for any whitespace followed by `@` (or detecting `@` that begins a JSDoc tag) rather than matching only `" @"`.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): map js default typedef aliases
candidates.body: Emitting the JSDoc `@typedef ... default` as `export type {alias_name} = ...` can produce an invalid `.d.ts` when `{alias_name}` already has a type declaration (e.g. a `class` creates a type). In the test case here, the output contains both `export type Cls = ...` and `declare class Cls ...`, which would trigger TS2300 (duplicate identifier) in TypeScript. Consider instead emitting a non-conflicting alias name and exporting it as a type-only default export (e.g. `export { type <alias> as default }`), or otherwise avoid generating a type alias that collides with an existing type name.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): map js default typedef aliases
candidates.body: This test currently expects output that includes both `export type Cls = ...` and `declare class Cls ...`. A class declaration introduces a type named `Cls`, so also emitting a type alias named `Cls` would make the generated `.d.ts` fail to type-check with TS2300 (duplicate identifier). If the goal is to model a JSDoc `default` typedef, consider asserting a non-conflicting representation (for example, a type-only default export via an export specifier) rather than a same-name type alias alongside a class.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): stabilize recursive Box2 fingerprint
candidates.body: This rewrite matches via `contains(...)` but then overwrites the entire `message_text`. If the TS2322 message ever includes additional elaboration lines (e.g. "Type ...\n  Property ..." patterns used elsewhere), this would silently drop that extra detail. Consider making the match stricter (e.g. equality/starts_with) and/or doing a targeted replacement of just the target display (replace "Box<number | Box2>" → "Box2") so any trailing detail is preserved.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align recursiveTypeReferences1 array diagnostics
candidates.body: The diagnostic suppression here is based only on exact `message_text` matches, so it will remove *any* TS2322 in this file with one of these messages, even if it comes from an unrelated assignment. To keep this rewrite from masking legitimate diagnostics, consider additionally narrowing the filter by span (e.g., only suppress when `diag.start` is within/near the known `flat/flat1/flat2` call sites, similar to the nearby-source check used in other rewrite helpers in this file).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align recursiveTypeReferences1 array diagnostics
candidates.body: This rewrite unconditionally suppresses the listed TS2322 messages once the top-level `source_text.contains(...)` guard passes, but it only re-adds the expected diagnostics if the specific `flat([1, ['a']]);` / `flat1...` / `flat2...` markers are found. If a source file happens to match the guard strings but not these call-site markers (or if the upstream conformance snippet changes), this will drop diagnostics without restoring them. Consider extending the initial guard to include the call markers, or only performing the suppression after confirming all three anchors exist.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): decorate system class exports
candidates.body: `system_class_needs_legacy_decorate_helper` only checks class decorators + ctor parameter decorators. Legacy member decorators (method/property/accessor) and method parameter decorators also emit `__decorate(...)` via `emit_legacy_member_decorator_calls`, so this pre-scan can skip inlining `__decorate` and generate System output that calls `__decorate` with no helper defined (runtime ReferenceError). Consider reusing the same member/parameter-decorator detection logic used in `emit_class_declaration` (class/emit_declaration.rs:44-101), and/or rely on the already-populated `transforms.helpers().decorate` instead of a shallow top-level scan so nested decorated classes (e.g. inside namespaces) are covered too.

candidates.pr_title: fix(emit): decorate system class exports
candidates.body: This clones the entire writer output (`get_output().to_string()`) just to slice out the newly-emitted segment, which is O(n) in the full file size and allocates twice. You can slice directly from `self.writer.get_output()` into an owned `String` before truncating to avoid copying the whole output buffer.

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): align indexSignatures1 fingerprints
candidates.body: `rewrite_index_signatures1_fingerprints` is invoked even when `ctx.allow_source_file_test_pragmas` is false, which means this conformance-specific diagnostic rewriting could affect normal CLI/LSP checking for any user file that happens to contain the trigger strings. Consider gating this call behind `allow_source_file_test_pragmas` (as is already done for `rewrite_intersection_index_signature_fingerprints`) to ensure fingerprint rewrites only run in conformance/test mode.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): ignore cjs exports in esm js modules
candidates.body: `source_file_has_native_esm_syntax` walks every top-level statement; it’s called multiple times during per-file setup (e.g., several `collect_js_*` helpers), which adds repeated O(n) passes over the same statement list. Consider computing this once per source file (store a boolean on `DeclarationEmitter` during setup and reuse it) to avoid extra scans on large JS files.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): ignore cjs exports in esm js modules
candidates.body: `source_file_has_native_esm_syntax` treats any `EXPORT_ASSIGNMENT` as “native ESM”, but in this codebase JS files can contain `export =` (see existing `test_js_export_equals_emits_before_target_declaration`). That means a JS file using `export =` will be misclassified as “native ESM” and will now skip CommonJS export synthesis/analysis guarded by this helper. Consider checking `EXPORT_ASSIGNMENT` nodes and only treating them as native ESM when they are *not* `export =` (i.e., `export default`), so `export =` JS files keep their CommonJS behavior.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): emit js function keyword property aliases
candidates.body: `reserved_names` is documented as tracking names taken in the *top-level scope* of the file. Inserting namespace member locals into this set (including when no alias is needed) can force unrelated later top-level imports/aliases to be renamed even though namespace members are scoped. Consider keeping a separate JS late-bound namespace-local name set (or only reserving within `reserved_member_names`) instead of mutating `self.reserved_names` here.

candidates.pr_title: fix(dts): emit js function keyword property aliases
candidates.body: When renaming a `namespace_member_name` due to a global collision, `generate_unique_name(namespace_member_name)` only checks `self.reserved_names`, not the already-known `reserved_member_names` for the current namespace. This can pick a name that exists as another member in the same namespace (e.g., choosing `normal_1` when there is already a `normal_1` property), producing duplicate declarations and an invalid `.d.ts`. Ensure the chosen synthetic name is unique against the current namespace’s member names as well (e.g., seed `reserved_names` with `reserved_member_names` before generating, or generate against both sets).

candidates.pr_title: fix(checker): map variadic tuple source display through rest slots
candidates.body: `target_tuple_element_for_literal_position` linearly scans `elements` to find the rest index on every literal element (`iter().position(...)`), which makes building a diagnostic O(literal_len * target_len) for large tuples. Consider computing `rest_index`/`suffix_len` once in `array_literal_tuple_source_type_display` (or caching it) and passing the precomputed mapping info into the per-element helper.
